name: The Ceasefire Holds
description: Commander Thorn has implemented Cross's revised assessment: eastern Wastes withdrawn from combat operations. Two months in, neither side has broken the informal ceasefire. Thorn wants eyes at the bone fields — the midpoint between the lines — to confirm what he suspects: the silence is real.
accept_text: He has the combat map on the table and for the first time in the years you've been coming to this office, the eastern sector has no red ink on it. No patrol lines, no threat assessments, no contact markers. Just the terrain. 'Two months,' he says. 'I pulled the eastern patrols back to the ridge line two months ago. Notified Cross through the back-channel. He pulled the Aetherian advance posts back the same week.' He looks at the map. 'Nothing has moved.' He pauses. 'What I'm about to say I haven't said to anyone yet, because I want to be sure first: I think the ceasefire is holding because both sides feel the same thing I feel when I look at that sector.' He looks at the map. 'The war has been about the Wastes because the Wastes are where the crystals are. The crystals are why both nations exist the way they do — the aetherium, the war economy, the strategic position. The Grid is why the crystals exist.' He looks at you. 'If the Grid reaches full coherence and the offer extends to everyone in range — if people begin to understand what the Wastes actually are — the war doesn't end because someone won. It ends because the premise that the war was being fought over dissolves.' He straightens. 'Go to the bone fields. Stand in the middle of the space between the lines. Tell me what you see. Tell me if it's quiet.' He pauses. 'Tell me if Theodric is still there.'
complete_text: The bone fields are quiet in a way they have not been in the years you've known this place. The usual distant sounds of the front — the report of artillery, the hum of Aetherian crystal weapons cycling — are absent. The field holds its particular silence, but the silence no longer has the quality of held breath. It has the quality of space. Theodric is here, moving through the field with his ledger, making his marks. He notices you are not alone in having come today: at the field's far edge, barely visible, there are two figures in Aetherian research livery standing at the tree line. They are not armed. They are looking at the field. Theodric has noticed them too. He nods in their direction when you reach him. 'Second time this week,' he says. 'Different people each time. They come to the edge, look at the field, don't cross, go back.' He makes a mark in the ledger. 'Yesterday an Aetherian archivist sent a letter to the monastery asking if there are records of the First Engagement from the Aetherian side that she might contribute to a documentation project.' He looks at you. 'Forty years she said she'd had those records. Forty years and she's sending them now.' He looks at the field. 'Something has changed.' He closes the ledger. 'I don't know yet if what's changed will last. But it has the quality of something that's been building for a while.' He looks at the far edge, where the Aetherian researchers have moved a few steps closer. 'This field has been tended for thirty-one years. It's always been my work alone.' He pauses. 'I think that's changing too.'
